speaker: interviewee
text: --Frieda Einsidler, the way I was known before I was married. And I just survived the Holocaust. It was two years. This was in 1940-- the early part of 1946. And it was already after the war. And I'm leaving Poland. I'm leaving Warsaw-- [POLISH], in Polish. And I-- I'm already with the other children who are about to go to-- to go through-- on a ship through the-- through the Baltic to England. And-- and I-- as I'm in Warsaw, the last day, the whole city was utter rubble. There was 90% destroyed. So we were in a part where there was hardly a building visible. And there were stones laying, bricks-- just rubble and bricks laying on the floor out of what's left of buildings. And we-- and I'm standing, just to go out of this building for a little while, in the fresh air, to wait for my ship-- to be taken to the ship. And as I'm standing, I see a photographer with a-- with a stand in the midst of the rubble, in the midst of the-- after the war, the Warsaw was destroyed. It was utterly destroyed. And there is a photographer standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what do I do? I ask him to take a picture of me. I had some money, and I gave him. And this is the picture-- the last picture as I left Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is picture-- on the sled is of Frieda Einsidler, after the war, in the winter of '45. And it's in Krakow-- [POLISH]-- and just before I left for Zabrze, which is another home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to say that I'm wearing pants here, because I was given pants to keep warm after the war. And I never wore pants again after that, because my father wouldn't allow me. But it was right after the war, and I feel I was allowed to wear pants to keep warm in the winter. This was taken in Warsaw-- [POLISH]-- after-- when I was-- we were leaving from Poland. And the lady there was a wonderful lady with her husband who-- who showed us great kindness. We slept in their house, waiting for the plane to take us to Gdansk. And I am in the pants on the bottom. I'm the-- with the white coat. That little boy next to me was a survivor, too. And I cannot remember his name. But the girl next to him was [NON-ENGLISH]. And she lives in London today. And the lady, her name was Mrs. Pupko. Her husband was a famous doctor who helped children. And he survived with his wife. And at that time, they had no children. So they sort of adopted us. And that little girl was [NON-ENGLISH]. She-- she-- she was also a survivor. She was very-- the smallest one, I believe, from our group. And-- and the-- the taller one was-- was Eva. And her name is, today, Eva Lieberman. And next to her is her sister, [NON-ENGLISH]. And they all-- all these were survivors from the Holocaust as we were leaving Poland from Warsaw to Gdansk. This is a picture of the three nuns in the convent who were in Grodzisko Dolne Gorne, because that was belonging to Gorne, where I was born in Dolne. These-- these nuns had a convent then. And the convent is on the same area, on the same ground, but a different house. And into this convent came a little Jewish child. She was the youngest baby to survive from our village. And these three nuns kept this child. And they didn't give it over to the German Gestapo-- whoever it was-- in our village. And they-- they protected this child. Her name was Hannah-- during the war, she was called Anna. But her real-- real name, actually, from home, I found out later it was Hannallah Kestenbaum. That was her name. And her grandfather had a butcher store in our village. And her father, I remember him. And the mother was the only one who survived. And after the war, this child went to church. And that's how she was saved, because they protected-- they pretended she was not Jewish. But, actually, she's the only little face among that group who had dark hair. All the others were non-Jewish children. And the nuns gave me this picture when I went back to Poland. And the nuns, today, are different nuns. But those nuns protected this child. The child went next to their habits, hidden. She was next to their habits, walking to church every Sunday. And they knew she was Jewish. And they protected her, and she survived. And her mother came and picked her up after the war. The picture here is in London. Most of them were various ages of young people who either came before the second world war from Germany or after the second world war from Poland. They were all-- their parents-- their parents did not survive. And it was in a hostel in Woodberry Down, London. And-- and I am in the middle. There is a little like mark where I'm-- I'm a little girl standing right in the middle. And the teacher, who was watching us, we should-- we shouldn't misbehave-- was Suzie. And I liked her very much. We all loved her. And she's standing next to me. And she lives in Israel today. OK. This is a picture of me, Frieda Stieglitz, and my husband, Israel Stieglitz. And we met--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we met here in America after the war, shortly after I came to America. And we were married here in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in 1951. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. In this picture, my father, Ephraim Einsidler, is sitting-- and my stepmother-- step mom-- and she was very nice to me. She brought me up. And after I came, my father married her after the war. And there is my daughter and son, Pinchas, and my daughter, Gloria. And son was married here in the picture. And-- and my husband is next to me. His name is Israel Stieglitz. OK. This is a picture of-- of Pini, my-- the son, and Sara. And the oldest is Racheli. And the next is Eli. And then come the twins, David and Jonathan-- David and Jonathan. OK. OK. This is a picture of my daughter, Gloria-- and her married name is Kestenbaum-- and her husband Richard Kestenbaum, and their two children. The older one is Aaroni. And the younger one is Alexandra. But they call her Alex. And the boy, they call Aaroni. But his name is Aaron. And they're two beautiful children. I'm very proud of them.
